topic: Optimal Chebyshev-spaced rollout measurement schedule and minimax linear-unbiased total-treatment-effect estimation under β-order network interference (Cortez et al. 2024, arXiv:2405.05119): over a Sobolev-ellipsoid β-order coefficient class, WHICH treated-fractions to roll out to is the design variable; the optimum is Chebyshev-spaced, and the minimax linear-unbiased estimator attains a worst-case design-variance β-exponent (C_s(c)/q)^{2β} with a matching lower bound from the classical Chebyshev extremal property — resolving whether Cortez et al.'s (β/q)^{2β} interpolation-variance penalty is intrinsic (exponential ORDER 2β fixed, BASE reducible via additional measurement points k=⌈cβ⌉)

open_problems.open_problem: For β-order network interference with a Sobolev-ellipsoid coefficient class, characterize the minimax linear-unbiased TTE estimator and the optimal k-point rollout treated-fraction schedule, rather than taking the β+1 equally spaced interpolation points as fixed.

source_refs.claim: Staggered rollout observations identify a β-degree polynomial in the treatment probability and give unbiased TTE estimators without network knowledge, but with interpolation variance.
source_refs.author: Cortez, Eichhorn, Yu

source_refs.claim: The two-stage rollout paper states that polynomial interpolation magnifies sampling variance by a factor of order (β/p)^{2β} and uses clustering to reduce the effective extrapolation distance.
source_refs.author: Cortez-Rodriguez, Eichhorn, Yu

source_refs.claim: Classical optimal-design theory links polynomial interpolation error and extremal-polynomial placement of support points, suggesting Chebyshev-type schedules but not proving the design-based network-interference theorem.
source_refs.author: Smith

source_refs.excerpt: minimize V(nu)=V0+V1*m2+V3*m3+V4*(m4-m2^2) over laws nu on [0,1]
The same-class UPPER endpoint remains an open residual

open_problems.why_unsolved: The rollout literature fixes the allocation nodes or changes the effective treatment budget through clustering, while the prior saturation-design proposal optimized a law over treated fractions but found that its first homogeneous model made the advertised higher-moment terms degenerate. The remaining obstacle is to combine the design-based randomization variance of finite-population rollout observations with a genuine smooth coefficient class and a continuous choice of rollout fractions; classical Chebyshev interpolation supplies the likely extremal geometry but not the causal TTE estimator, unbiasedness class, or finite-population lower bound.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A minimax theorem over all linear unbiased estimators and all k-point rollout schedules, proving the Chebyshev-spaced schedule and its estimator attain the sharp worst-case design-variance exponent over the β-order Sobolev ellipsoid, with a matching lower bound.

writing_exemplar.why: It motivates the exact variance explosion from polynomial extrapolation and frames rollout fractions as operational design choices under network interference.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Network interference makes ordinary A/B estimators biased -> rollout observations turn β-order TTE into polynomial extrapolation -> equally spaced low-budget nodes create exponential variance -> choose nodes optimally under a minimax design criterion

method_exemplars.technique: Chebyshev alternation and extremal-polynomial arguments for optimal support points in polynomial interpolation, adapted to the rollout TTE functional.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired lower bound is an extremal-polynomial statement for evaluating F(1)-F(0) from noisy values at chosen treated fractions.

method_exemplars.technique: Polynomial-interpolation construction of a linear unbiased TTE estimator from staggered rollout means and its design-variance calculation.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the estimator class and variance object that the Chebyshev schedule would optimize.

method_exemplars.technique: Moment-program optimization of randomized saturation designs and variance formulas under interference, including finite-cluster implementation constraints.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It treats treatment fractions themselves as the experimental design variable, matching the rollout schedule choice.

open_problems.open_problem: Under β-order interference with possible clustering, jointly characterize the optimal graph partition/subpopulation rule and the optimal rollout treated-fraction grid for TTE estimation.

source_refs.claim: The two-stage rollout paper reports a tension between clustering objectives that cut few network edges and objectives that balance covariates, while variance depends on clustering qualities and interpolation.
source_refs.author: Cortez-Rodriguez, Eichhorn, Yu

source_refs.claim: Graph cluster randomization uses network partitions to change exposure probabilities and reduce interference bias, but does not optimize rollout measurement nodes.
source_refs.author: Ugander, Karrer, Backstrom, Kleinberg

source_refs.claim: Network experiment design is framed as a tradeoff between bias reduction through graph clustering and precision, leaving saturation-node optimization outside the design criterion.
source_refs.author: Eckles, Karrer, Ugander

source_refs.excerpt: strict nonsharpness and cut-certificate statements are conjectures and are the real novelty kernel

open_problems.why_unsolved: Cluster-randomized network designs optimize graph exposure structure, while β-order rollout estimators optimize information about a polynomial in treatment saturation. Cortez-Rodriguez et al. explicitly expose the conflict between edge-cut and covariate-balance clustering, and the prior network-exposure proposal leaves the shared hidden-network constraints as conjectural; neither line gives a single risk criterion in which graph cuts, selected subpopulation size q, and the rollout measurement nodes are optimized together.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A design theorem or algorithm that chooses clusters/subpopulations and rollout fractions jointly, with a worst-case risk bound decomposing graph-cut bias, randomization variance, and Chebyshev/interpolation design variance.

writing_exemplar.why: Its narrative already contrasts network-free rollouts with clustering-assisted variance reduction and names the unresolved clustering tradeoff.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Rollout estimators avoid network knowledge -> clustering can raise effective treatment saturation but introduces cut-edge bias -> clustering objectives conflict -> solve cluster choice and saturation-node choice in one design theorem

method_exemplars.technique: Exposure-probability accounting under graph cluster randomization and graph-partition objectives.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The joint theorem needs the same partition-to-exposure map before adding rollout-node optimization.

method_exemplars.technique: Bias and variance decomposition for a clustered two-stage rollout polynomial estimator.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Its decomposition supplies the terms that a joint cluster-and-node criterion would sharpen.

open_problems.open_problem: For randomized saturation or rollout designs with heterogeneous unit-level spillover parameters, derive the nondegenerate optimal law over treated fractions and its finite-cluster rounding loss when third- and fourth-moment variance terms are live.

source_refs.claim: Randomized saturation designs choose treatment proportions across clusters; under interference, neither stratified nor cluster-based designs are generally optimal, and the paper optimizes bias/variance under a linear interference model.
source_refs.author: Cai, Pouget-Abadie, Airoldi

source_refs.excerpt: heterogenize to Cai's unit-level gamma_i model so V3,V4 are non-degenerate

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published saturation-design paper supplies the design variable and linear-interference variance formulas, but the prior CausalSmith formalization found that its first homogeneous specialization collapses V3 and V4 to zero, making the skew/kurtosis design headline vacuous. The concrete obstacle is to move to a heterogeneous unit-level spillover model in which the quartic moment program is actually live and then prove exact optimality and finite implementation rather than only invoking a generic moment-program scaffold.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem deriving live V3 and V4 coefficients from a heterogeneous spillover model, proving support-at-most-three/KKT optimality of the treated-fraction law, and bounding the rounding loss for finitely many clusters and within-cluster units.

writing_exemplar.why: It gives the cleanest motivation arc for treating saturation proportions as optimizable design objects under interference.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Randomized saturation designs interpolate between stratified and cluster randomization -> under interference classical extremes need not be optimal -> variance depends on moments of the saturation law -> characterize the exact optimal law in the heterogeneous regime

method_exemplars.technique: Closed-form bias/variance derivation for randomized saturation designs under linear interference.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The open theorem must derive the same kind of variance functional before optimizing the law over treated fractions.

method_exemplars.technique: Extreme-point support bounds for probability measures under moment constraints, used to reduce the quartic design problem to finitely many atoms.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired support-at-most-three optimizer is a moment-space extreme-point statement.

open_problems.open_problem: Can a rollout measurement schedule adapt to unknown β, Sobolev radius, or approximate-interference decay while retaining near-minimax TTE risk and valid design-based inference?

source_refs.claim: Approximate neighborhood interference weakens exact neighborhood restrictions by allowing smaller but nonzero effects from distant alters, so fixed-radius exposure assumptions can be misspecified.
source_refs.author: Leung

source_refs.claim: Unknown interference can still allow generalized average-effect estimation under limited interference, but conventional confidence statements may fail.
source_refs.author: Sävje, Aronow, Hudgens

source_refs.claim: Neighborhood-adaptive estimators search over relevant radii and patterns under unknown interference, proving oracle inequalities and asymptotic normality for causal effects.
source_refs.author: Belloni, Fang, Volfovsky

source_refs.claim: β is the structural complexity parameter that drives the interpolation estimator and its variance under low-order network interactions.
source_refs.author: Cortez-Rodriguez, Eichhorn, Yu

open_problems.why_unsolved: The rollout estimator requires choosing β and a measurement plan before randomization, but approximate- and unknown-interference papers emphasize that the relevant order, radius, or decay envelope is typically not known. Adaptive-interference estimators search over exposure patterns for fixed designs, while Chebyshev-like minimax rollout schedules are tuned to a smoothness/order class; the missing piece is an honest adaptive schedule with an oracle inequality and post-selection inference under assignment randomness.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An adaptive or universal rollout schedule with an oracle risk bound over β and Sobolev/decay classes, plus a design-based confidence procedure whose coverage is uniform over the same model collection.

writing_exemplar.why: It provides the motivation for replacing exact low-order assumptions by decay/approximation classes.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Exact neighborhood interference is often unrealistic -> approximate interference controls misspecification by decay -> rollout variance depends on the assumed order β -> construct an adaptive schedule and inference rule over orders

method_exemplars.technique: Lepski-style search over interference radii with oracle inequalities and asymptotic-normality arguments after data-driven pattern selection.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The adaptive rollout schedule needs the same bias-variance/order-selection logic, but for saturation nodes rather than exposure neighborhoods.

method_exemplars.technique: Approximate-neighborhood-interference assumptions translating decaying distant effects into estimable exposure effects.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the model class over which β-order rollout adaptivity would be judged.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend Chebyshev/minimax rollout design from static saturation-indexed potential outcomes to dynamic staggered adoption with time trends, carryover, and network exposure histories.

source_refs.claim: Partial-interference experiments define direct, indirect, total, and overall effects under group-contained interference, but do not solve network rollout timing with carryover.
source_refs.author: Hudgens, Halloran

source_refs.claim: Time-series experiments require potential outcomes indexed by treatment paths, showing how carryover and temporal assignment complicate design-based causal inference.
source_refs.author: Bojinov, Shephard

source_refs.claim: Experiment roll-outs use temporal variation in treatment fractions to model interference, but the paper focuses on model selection and identification conditions rather than minimax node/time design.
source_refs.author: Boyarsky, Namkoong, Pouget-Abadie

source_refs.claim: The β-order rollout estimator is organized around a cross-sectional polynomial in treatment saturation, leaving time-history exposure outside the minimax design problem.
source_refs.author: Cortez-Rodriguez, Eichhorn, Yu

open_problems.why_unsolved: Static β-order rollout design has one design axis, the treated fraction. Dynamic staggered adoption adds measurement times, duration under treatment, calendar trends, and network exposure histories; Chebyshev interpolation in saturation does not by itself separate spillovers from carryover or secular trends. Existing time-series and roll-out modeling papers handle path-indexed potential outcomes or model selection, but not a minimax linear-unbiased design over a bivariate time-saturation smoothness class.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A two-dimensional design theorem over saturation nodes and measurement times, giving a linear unbiased estimator or impossibility result for a dynamic TTE and matching minimax bounds under smoothness in saturation and carryover time.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames the core move from static treatment indicators to treatment-path potential outcomes in design-based experiments.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Static causal effects ignore treatment histories -> time-series experiments require path-indexed potential outcomes -> network rollout adds saturation histories -> choose time and saturation nodes for minimax TTE estimation

method_exemplars.technique: Potential-outcome indexing by treatment paths and design-based estimands for temporal experiments.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The dynamic rollout theorem needs a path-indexed estimand before adding network saturation.

method_exemplars.technique: Use temporal variation in roll-out assignments to learn interference models and estimands.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest rollout-specific method paper for exploiting variation over calendar time.

method_exemplars.technique: Tensorizing Chebyshev extremal grids from a one-dimensional interpolation problem to a time-by-saturation design surface.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The proposed bivariate minimax lower bound would likely reduce to product or anisotropic extremal-polynomial structure.

literature_map:
Cortez, Eichhorn, and Yu 2022 introduced network-agnostic staggered rollout estimators for TTE using the polynomial relation between expected outcome and treatment probability.
Cortez-Rodriguez, Eichhorn, and Yu 2024/AISTATS 2025 show that interpolation can magnify variance by an order like (β/p)^{2β} and use a clustered two-stage rollout to reduce effective extrapolation.
The topic's main gap is that the rollout treated fractions are usually fixed or equally spaced, while the design problem should optimize the support points themselves.
Smith 1918 is the classical optimal-design/interpolation anchor suggesting Chebyshev extremal geometry, but it is not a design-based network-interference theorem.
Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi 2022 treat saturation proportions as design variables under interference and provide variance formulas, but not β-order rollout interpolation/minimax schedules.
Ugander et al. 2013 and Eckles et al. 2017 optimize graph-cluster exposure designs; Cortez-Rodriguez et al. 2024 show clustering creates a bias-variance tradeoff with covariate balance.
Aronow-Samii style exposure mapping and Sävje-Aronow-Hudgens unknown-interference results motivate design-based validity when exposure structure is limited but not fully known.
Leung 2022 ANI and Belloni-Fang-Volfovsky 2022 show that exact interference radius/order is often unknown, raising adaptivity and honest-inference questions for rollout schedules.
Han-Li-Mao-Wu 2022 and Boyarsky-Namkoong-Pouget-Abadie 2023 use increasing allocation/roll-out variation to detect or model interference, but not to solve minimax TTE node placement.
Hudgens-Halloran 2008 and Bojinov-Shephard 2019 point to dynamic and partial-interference complications when treatment paths and carryover matter.
The strongest cross-track links are optimal treated-fraction law design, network-cluster rollout design, and same-class minimax upper/lower endpoints for smooth causal functionals.
The surviving open problems therefore center on Chebyshev/minimax rollout nodes, joint cluster-and-node design, heterogeneous saturation moment programs, adaptivity to unknown β, and dynamic time-saturation rollout schedules.
prior_proposal_map:
Track A mined doc/formalization/_bank plus research and restart/archive proposal material for experimentation, network-interference, rollout, minimax, and saturation-design signals.
The closest design-variable prior was doc/formalization/_bank/downgraded/exp_saturation_skew_threshold_v1, whose README asks to optimize a law over treated fractions but reports that the homogeneous model made V3,V4 degenerate and should be re-raised with heterogeneous spillovers.
The closest network-interference prior was pid_network_exposure_v1, where strict nonsharpness and cut-certificate statements about shared hidden-network completion remained conjectural.
The accepted stat_dose_response_minimax_holder_anisotropic_converse README provides a same-class minimax lower-bound substrate and explicitly leaves the matching upper endpoint open.
Several failed rollout/switchback/frontier entries warned against tautological row-space or contrast-matrix frontiers, motivating direct derivation of estimator loadings and variance rather than defining the frontier by construction.
No prior proposal already proves the Chebyshev-spaced β-order rollout minimax theorem; the prior artefacts mainly supply adjacent open problems and anti-patterns for schedule-design formalization.
